---
title: "About"
---

I'm lucky to wear two hats; one as a Ph.D. candidate in Sociology at Stanford and the other of a research science intern at Intuit.  In both of these roles I get the chance to do what I love, which is to work with big, messy data and produce insightful analyses and research. My academic research and professional experiences in the tech and data space have allowed me to work with different types of data, including satellite imagery, U.S. Census data, state voter records, social media data, and workplace communication data (e.g., Zoom, Slack, Outlook). Working with different types of data and telling stories with data the common themes running through my academic research and professional experiences. 

As a Ph.D. candidate, much of my time is focused on running analyses and conducting research for my dissertation. My dissertation is concerned with what makes vibrant and attractive public spaces that draw people from a variety of backgrounds and neighborhoods. I draw on satellite imagery, foot-traffic data, and U.S. Census data and apply geospatial data science and statistical methods to build my arguments. You can see some of my work in my [portfolio](portfolio.html).

My fascination with human mobility and what makes vibrant public spaces stems from my own experience living in different parts of the country and comparing the physical landscapes, land uses, and public spaces across different regions. My geographic biography is highlighted in the map below. I grew up in a region of south-central Texas called the Texas Hill Country, which lies between San Antonio and Austin. My first major move was to Philadelphia to complete my undergraduate degree in Sociology at the University of Pennsylvania. After completing my bachelor’s degree, I swapped coasts to move to Palo Alto for the opportunity pursue a Ph.D. in Sociology at Stanford University. A few months after the Covid-19 pandemic began, I moved back to south-central Texas for one year to help my parents while continuing my studies. I recently moved back to the San Francisco Peninsula to finish out my Ph.D. and begin my career as a geospatial scientist.

[INSERT MAP ABOUT HERE]


In the last two years I have sought out opportunities outside of academia to grow my data analytics and storytelling skills. Because I enjoy *solving* geospatial problems like site selection, resource allocation, and mobility trends, I envision my career in geospatial science taking place largely in industry and outside of the academy where I can apply my knowledge of spatial features and spatial relationships; architect and build GIS networks and data models; validate or vet output from the ever-easier-to-use desktop and web tools; and be a data steward in organizations where geographic information counts as a source of data.
